paper_title: Reduction of carbon dioxide with water under concentrated sunlight using photocatalyst combined with Fe-based catalyst

catalyst.source: Reduction of carbon dioxide with water under concentrated sunlight using photocatalyst combined with Fe-based catalyst
catalyst.context: Reduction of carbon dioxide with water under concentrated sunlight using photocatalyst combined with Fe-based catalyst

catalyst.source: The reduction of CO 2 with water into valuable organic compounds under concentrated sunlight was investigated for the first time over a hybrid catalyst, in which a Pt-loaded K 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst was combined with a Fe-based catalyst supported on a dealuminated Y-type zeolite (Fe-Cu-KDAY).
catalyst.context: The reduction of CO 2 with water into valuable organic compounds under concentrated sunlight was investigated for the first time over a hybrid catalyst, in which a Pt-loaded K 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst was combined with a Fe-based catalyst supported on a dealuminated Y-type zeolite (Fe-Cu-KDAY).In this reaction system, the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 catalyst decomposes water to produce H 2 and the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst reduces CO 2 with the resulting H 2 into organic compounds.When only the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst was used, organic compounds such as CH 4 , HCOOH, and HCHO were formed together with H 2 , indicating that CO 2 can also be directly reduced over PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 .On the contrary, when the hybrid catalyst was used, CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH were produced in addition to the above products, depending on the reaction temperature and catalyst composition.The reaction temperature significantly increased by concentrating the solar irradiation, reaching 600 K and increasing the product yields.It is suggested that such a high reaction temperature facilitates both the photoreduction and hydrogenation of CO 2 over PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 and Fe-Cu-KDAY, respectively.

catalyst.source: The prepared Fe-Cu-KDAY and PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 powders were physically mixed by grinding (hereafter, referred to as hybrid catalyst).
catalyst.context: The chemicals used in the present study were of reagent grade, purchased from Wako Pure Chemical Industry, and used without further purification.The dealuminized Y-type zeolite (DAY zeolite), with a SiAl ratio of 11.4, was prepared by steam dealumination of a commercial Y-type zeolite (Catalysts Chemicals Industry Co. Ltd., Japan), which was ion exchanged with NH 4 prior to use.The Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst with the composition of 1.0Fe:0.03Cu:0.5K:11.0DAY (weight ratio) was prepared by impregnation.DAY zeolite powders were impregnated in a mixed solution of metal nitrites at the designated ratio while stirring.The resulting powder samples were then dried overnight at 373 K, followed by calcination at 673 K for 2 h in a stream of O 2 at a flow rate of 30 mlmin, and subsequently reduced at 673 K for 2 h in H 2 (20 mlmin). The Pt-loaded K 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst was synthesized as follows: 5.0 g of TiO 2 (P-25) powder was dispersed in distilled water containing 1.4 g of K 2 CO 3 at room temperature.The resulting samples were dried and then calcined at 1213 K for 20 h in air.Pt was loaded on the K 2 Ti 6 O 13 powder by photochemical deposition.3.0 g of K 2 Ti 6 O 13 powder was dispersed in 60 ml of 2 M Na 2 CO 3 solution containing 0.019 g of H 2 PtCl 6 .The above suspension was exposed to the light of a 150 W Hg-lamp, resulting in the photochemical deposition of Pt on K 2 Ti 6 O 13 .Finally, the Pt-loaded K 2 Ti 6 O 13 powders were thoroughly rinsed with distilled water. The prepared Fe-Cu-KDAY and PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 powders were physically mixed by grinding (hereafter, referred to as hybrid catalyst).The prepared catalysts were analyzed using an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, RINT-1400) with Cu K radiation as well as a scanning electron microscope (Hitachi, S-5200).Diffuse reflection spectra were recorded using a UV-Vis spectrometer (Jasco V-550).The BET surface area of the sample powders was measured by the nitrogen adsorption method. The photoreduction experiments of CO 2 with water under Xe-or Hg-lamp irradiation at room temperature were carried out using a photoreactor (173 cm 3 ) equipped with a 63.3 cm 2 flat quartz window.The catalysts were highly dispersed on a filter paper fixed on the bottom of the reactor, into which an appropriate amount of distilled water was introduced.The filter paper acted as a wet bed for the catalyst layer.After degassing using a vacuum system, highly purified CO 2 was introduced into the reactor, which was externally irradiated through the window by a 300 W Xe-lamp and a 150 W Hg-lamp, respectively. Fig. 1 shows a schematic drawing of the experimental system for the photoreduction of CO 2 with water under concentrated sunlight.As illustrated in Fig. 1, the prepared catalysts were placed on wet quartz wool in an optical quartz tube cell (reaction cell), and subsequently an appropriate amount of distilled water was introduced into the cell.In this case, the quartz wool was used as a wet bed for the catalyst layer.After the reaction cell was deaerated using a vacuum system, highly purified CO 2 was introduced into the cell.The cell was tightly closed using a perfluororubber O-ring and no organic contaminants were detected in blank tests performed without the addition of CO 2 .The cell was set on a support bar fixed on an equatorial mount (GPD-type, Vixen, Japan) driven by a sky sensor, which can track the sun automatically.In order to focus the sunlight on the catalyst layer, a concave mirror (diameter 200 mm) was also fixed on the support bar so that the catalyst layer was positioned at the focal point of the mirror.The reaction temperature was monitored with a thermocouple, which was connected to an NR-1000 data acquisition system (Keyence, Japan) and a laptop computer.The experiments were done between 9:30 and 15:30 h on sunny days.Solar insolation upon the reaction cell was measured with a pyronometer (Ishikawa Sangyou Co. Ltd., Japan) H 2 and CH 4 produced in the gas phase were determined using a gas chromatograph (GC) (Shimadzu, GC-14B) equipped with a molecular sieve 5 A column and detected by a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and a flame ionization detector (FID), respectively.Ar was used as the carrier gas.HCOOH produced in the liquid phase was determined using a GC (Shimadzu, GC-6A) equipped with a PEG-6000 column and a TCD, while HCHO was determined using a Porapak T column and a TCD.CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH were determined using a GC (Shimadzu, GC-17A) equipped with a capillary column and an FID.He was used as the carrier gas for the analysis of the products in the liquid phase.The amount of samples taken from the liquid phase for the GC analysis was 10 l.

catalyst.source: The PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst was composed of rod-like crystallites (Fig. 2b).
catalyst.context: Scanning electron microscope photographs of the prepared catalysts are shown in Fig. 2. The Fe-Cu-K DAY crystals were aggregated into cubic particles of ca.0.5-2 m in size (Fig. 2a).The PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst was composed of rod-like crystallites (Fig. 2b).Fig. 2c shows the morphology of the physical mixture of the two catalysts in a 1:1 weight ratio (hybrid catalyst).As can be seen, the form of the two individual crystals remain unchanged, but the particle size of the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst became smaller due to the grinding, and the surface of the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst was partly covered with the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst. The crystallinity of the prepared samples was examined by XRD analysis.As illustrated in Fig. 3, due to the formation of Fe, Cu, and K components in the cavity as well as on the outer surface of the DAY zeolite, the peak intensity of the DAY zeolite significantly decreased.The BET specific surface areas of the DAY zeolite and Fe-Cu-KDAY were 523.3 and 314.5 m 2 g, respectively, also indicating that the DAY zeolite crystals were covered with the amorphous metal oxide layers.In this case, the DAY zeolite was used as the catalyst support, providing a sufficient surface area for the active metals to disperse and preventing them from sintering.In addition, the OH groups in the DAY zeolite were expected to promote CO 2 uptake on the catalyst.As for the hybrid catalyst, the peak of Fe-Cu-KDAY is hardly seen because the crystallinity of K 2 Ti 6 O 13 is much higher than that of Fe-Cu-KDAY. The light absorption properties of the prepared catalysts were also investigated.Fig. 4 shows the diffuse reflectance spectra of K 2 Ti 6 O 13 (a), the Pt-loaded with Fe-Cu-KDAY has no significant effect on the light absorption behavior of K 2 Ti 6 O 13 , because the absorption edge of the hybrid catalyst is shifted to neither the blue nor the red as compared with that of K 2 Ti 6 O 13 .Therefore, it is considered that the prepared hybrid catalyst can produce at least H 2 from water under UV-irradiation, as previously investigated 35. Table 1 shows the results of the photoreduction of CO 2 with water under Xe-or Hg-lamp irradiation.Each experiment was carried out using 0.3 g of catalyst with 77 kPa of CO 2 and 4.0 ml of distilled water for 6 h in the photoreactor described earlier at room temperature.As shown in Table 1, no reaction products were detected when the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst was exposed to the UV-irradiation from the Hg-lamp.It is suggested that the prepared Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst has no photocatalytic activity for the reduction of CO 2 with water.On the contrary, the evolution of H 2 and CH 4 in the gas phase as well as HCOOH and HCHO in the liquid phase were confirmed for the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst under Hg-lamp irradiation.However, when irradiated with a Xe-lamp, only the evolution of H 2 and CH 4 was observed for PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 .As considered above, this is because the prepared PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst has photocatalytic reactivity in the wavelength regions of 300-420 nm under Xe-lamp irradiation, and wavelengths longer than 420 nm cannot be utilized for the reaction.Note that 33,34.This may account for the reason why no detectable amount of O 2 was formed in this study.On the other hand, the yields of products decreased when the hybrid catalyst was used under both Xe-and Hg-lamp irradiation.The decrease in activity observed here might be due to the light shadowing effect of the Fe-Cu-KDAY powders on the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 powders in the hybrid catalyst, because the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst has no photocatalytic activity and the light incident on Fe-Cu-KDAY is not utilized for the photocatalytic reaction, but lost as heat.It should be also taken into consideration that for Fe-based catalysts, the CO 2 hydrogenation proceeds via the conversion of iron into iron carbide (mainly composed of the Hagg carbide, X-Fe 5 C 2 ) in the presence of CO 2 .However, a relatively high temperature (ca.573 K) is necessary for this conversion 2122232425262728, thus, at room temperature, the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst shows no effects on the reduction of CO 2 .We next performed the reduction of CO 2 with water over the hybrid catalyst using concentrated sunlight, which can supply photons and thermal energy required for the water decomposition and CO 2 hydrogenation over the hybrid catalyst to occur. As mentioned earlier, Fe-based catalysts exhibit catalytic activity for the CO 2 hydrogenation only at relatively high temperatures.We first investigated the effect of temperature on the reduction of CO 2 with water over the hybrid catalyst using Fe-Cu-KDAY and PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 in a 1:1 weight ratio under concentrated sunlight.Table 2 shows the obtained results for various months.In addition, Fig. 5 shows the variations in temperature in the reaction cell under concentrated sunlight with respect to the various experimental days noted in Table 2. Some probabilistic factors, such as wind velocity and humidity, affect the intensity of solar energy received by the cell, resulting in the fluctuation of the temperature in the cell during the experimental periods.It should be noted that the maximum temperature reached ca.600 K in October and 540 K in December.At such relatively high temperatures, the catalytic activity, especially of the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst, would be enhanced to a much greater extent than that at room temperature.Apparently, as shown in Table 2, the species and yields of organic products increased as compared to the results shown in Table 1.When the average reaction temperature was 534 K, HCHO and CH 4 were formed, while traces of HCOOH and CH 3 OH were detected.However, as the average temperature increased to 562 K, the yields of HCOOH, CH 3 OH, and C 2 H 5 OH significantly increased.Moreover, at 590 K, these yields increased two times over those obtained at 562 K. Likewise, the yield of CH 4 also increased with the increasing reaction temperature.Since a concave mirror was used in this study to concentrate the sunlight, both the thermal and photon energy of the sunlight might simultaneously increase, and concentrated sunlight was expected to more efficiently facilitate the reduction of CO 2 into organic compounds than the normal sunlight.The increase in the yields observed here might be due to the heat-enhanced reduction of CO 2 over the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst as well as the photo-enhanced hydrogenation of CO 2 over the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst under concentrated sunlight.In the case of a photocatalyst, in general, light irradiation is the primary source of the energy for the formation of electron-hole pairs at ambient temperature, because the band gap energy is too high to be overcome by thermal excitation.However, at a higher temperature, an increase in the collision frequency and diffusion rate can lead to an increase in the reaction rate.Fujii and Matsuda 36 reported that boiling the reaction solution by heating at 373 K led to a remarkable improvement in the activity of the photocatalytic decomposition of water.These findings indicate that the photocatalytic activity could be enhanced by supplying heat.On the other hand, in the case of the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst, which is not a semiconductor, like other FT iron-based catalysts, its catalytic activity is basically enhanced by heat.However, its reaction activity is considered to be also improved by light irradiation because the ultraviolet photon having relatively high energy, which is included in sunlight, can be absorbed by the valence electrons of any materials, which may assist in generating electronic defect sites for valence level excitation of the adsorbed reactants.Consequently, the yields of organic compounds remarkably increased under concentrated sunlight in the present study. Comparative tests were also conducted on the reduction of CO 2 with water over the hybrid catalyst using Fe-Cu-KDAY and PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 in various weight ratios under concentrated sunlight.As discussed previously, many factors affect the intensity of the solar energy received by the catalysts, depending on experimental days, which in turn result in variations in the average reaction temperature.Hence, we used the experimental results having the average reaction temperature of 579-590 K for comparisons from all the obtained results.The extracted data are shown in Table 3.It is noticeable that the yields of CH 4 and HCOOH and HCHO are higher at the reaction temperature of 583 K for the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst than those at the room temperature shown in Table 1.It also indicates that the photoreduction activity of PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 increased with the increasing reaction temperature.However, no CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH were detected even at a high temperature over PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 . From these results, we can conclude that CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH are catalytically formed over the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst.On the contrary, when only the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst was used, no product was detected even after the prolonged irradiation of concentrated sunlight.It follows that H 2 as the reducing agent is indispensable for the CO 2 reduction over the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst.We also observed that the decrease in CH 4 , HCOOH, and HCHO yields for the hybrid catalysts occurred with the increase in the weight ratio of Fe-Cu-KDAY to PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 , as shown in Table 3.As stated in the previous section, the shadowing effect of the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst on the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst would prevail when increasing the weight ratio of Fe-Cu-KDAY in the hybrid catalyst.This means that the number of incident photons reaching K 2 Ti 6 O 13 becomes less for the hybrid catalyst, thereby reducing the number of electrons and holes available for the reaction.Therefore, the decrease in the yields of the photoreduction products, including H 2 , CH 4 , HCOOH and HCHO, over PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 is responsible for the decrease in the yields for the hybrid catalyst.Inui and coworkers 25,26 have reported that the Fe-based catalysts exhibited high catalytic reactivity for the formation of C C bond on its surface, and that alcohols such as CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH were always easily formed in the CO 2 hydrogenation process.In the present study, DAY was used as a catalyst support instead of Al 2 O 3 .As discussed earlier in terms of the results shown in Tables 123, we found that the combination of Fe-Cu-KDAY with PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 led to the formation of CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH under concentrated sunlight.However, if the weight ratio of Fe-Cu-KDAY in the hybrid catalyst inappropriately increased (e.g.2:1 weight ratio in Table 3), the formation of CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH could be suppressed, because a serious shadowing effect of the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst may result in no H 2 evolution over the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst, or an insufficient amount of H 2 produced for the CO 2 hydrogenation over the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst.Moreover, it is noteworthy that the reaction temperature reached in the present study was relatively low in comparison with that reported by Inui and coworkers 25,26.We rationalize here the enhanced catalytic activity of the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst as follows: (i) DAY promotes CO 2 dissociative chemisorption on the Fe-Cu-KDAY surface because of the OH groups adsorbed in DAY, (ii) iron carbide, presumably reduction sites for CO 2 , is more easily formed and activated under the irradiation of concentrated sunlight than in the dark due to the photo-enhancement effect as mentioned earlier, and (iii) H 2 for the CO 2 hydrogenation is provided by the water decomposition over the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst.It is well known that the H 2 spillover phenomenon forming active atomic H easily occurs on the Pt metals loaded on a catalyst 37.Therefore, it is possible that atomic H formed on Pt, which is loaded on K 2 Ti 6 O 13 , moves to the neighboring surface of Fe-Cu-KDAY with reduced difficulty.This may assist in decreasing thermal energy needed for the CO 2 hydrogenation reaction over the hybrid catalyst; accordingly, CO 2 is reduced to CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH under the rather mild conditions employed in this study. In order to investigate in more detail the effect of the combination of the two catalysts, Fe-Cu-KDAY and PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 , on the reduction of CO 2 with water, the two catalysts were packed in series inside the reactor cell in a 1:1 weight ratio.As a result, the yields of CH 4 , HCOOH, and HCHO increased, but those of CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH decreased compared to that obtained when physically mixed, as shown in Table 3.This indicates that the shadowing effect of Fe-Cu-KDAY on PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 disappears when packed in series, but the transport of the active species, atomic H, resulting from H 2 spillover on PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 to Fe-Cu-KDAY becomes difficult. The obtained results revealed that the combination of the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst and the Fe-Cu-K DAY CO 2 hydrogenation catalyst was effective for synthesizing CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH from CO 2 and water under concentrated sunlight.However, the amount of obtained organic products was low; the apparent turnover number (TON), defined as the ratio of the total amount of CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH produced to that of the active site of Fe in the hybrid catalyst was 0.05 when used at 590 K for 6 h.This low TON value observed in this study must be due to the low activity of the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst for H 2 production, though the TON for H 2 production (the amount of H 2 formed to that of Pt in the hybrid catalyst) exceeded unity.Thus, more active photocatalysts, which is able to supply a sufficient amount of H 2 for the CO 2 hydrogenation over Fe-Cu-KDAY to proceed at high rate, should be developed to improve the CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH yields in this reaction system.

catalyst.source: The reduction of CO 2 with water under concentrated sunlight over the hybrid catalyst, in which the Pt-loaded K 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst was combined with the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst, resulted in the formation of organic compounds such as CH 4 , HCOOH, HCHO, CH 3 OH, and C 2 H 5 OH.

light source.source: Reduction of carbon dioxide with water under concentrated sunlight using photocatalyst combined with Fe-based catalyst
light source.context: Reduction of carbon dioxide with water under concentrated sunlight using photocatalyst combined with Fe-based catalyst

light source.source: The reduction of CO 2 with water into valuable organic compounds under concentrated sunlight was investigated for the first time over a hybrid catalyst, in which a Pt-loaded K 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst was combined with a Fe-based catalyst supported on a dealuminated Y-type zeolite (Fe-Cu-KDAY).
light source.context: The reduction of CO 2 with water into valuable organic compounds under concentrated sunlight was investigated for the first time over a hybrid catalyst, in which a Pt-loaded K 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst was combined with a Fe-based catalyst supported on a dealuminated Y-type zeolite (Fe-Cu-KDAY).In this reaction system, the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 catalyst decomposes water to produce H 2 and the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst reduces CO 2 with the resulting H 2 into organic compounds.When only the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst was used, organic compounds such as CH 4 , HCOOH, and HCHO were formed together with H 2 , indicating that CO 2 can also be directly reduced over PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 .On the contrary, when the hybrid catalyst was used, CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH were produced in addition to the above products, depending on the reaction temperature and catalyst composition.The reaction temperature significantly increased by concentrating the solar irradiation, reaching 600 K and increasing the product yields.It is suggested that such a high reaction temperature facilitates both the photoreduction and hydrogenation of CO 2 over PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 and Fe-Cu-KDAY, respectively.

light source.source: Fig. 1 shows a schematic drawing of the experimental system for the photoreduction of CO 2 with water under concentrated sunlight.
light source.context: The chemicals used in the present study were of reagent grade, purchased from Wako Pure Chemical Industry, and used without further purification.The dealuminized Y-type zeolite (DAY zeolite), with a SiAl ratio of 11.4, was prepared by steam dealumination of a commercial Y-type zeolite (Catalysts Chemicals Industry Co. Ltd., Japan), which was ion exchanged with NH 4 prior to use.The Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst with the composition of 1.0Fe:0.03Cu:0.5K:11.0DAY (weight ratio) was prepared by impregnation.DAY zeolite powders were impregnated in a mixed solution of metal nitrites at the designated ratio while stirring.The resulting powder samples were then dried overnight at 373 K, followed by calcination at 673 K for 2 h in a stream of O 2 at a flow rate of 30 mlmin, and subsequently reduced at 673 K for 2 h in H 2 (20 mlmin). The Pt-loaded K 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst was synthesized as follows: 5.0 g of TiO 2 (P-25) powder was dispersed in distilled water containing 1.4 g of K 2 CO 3 at room temperature.The resulting samples were dried and then calcined at 1213 K for 20 h in air.Pt was loaded on the K 2 Ti 6 O 13 powder by photochemical deposition.3.0 g of K 2 Ti 6 O 13 powder was dispersed in 60 ml of 2 M Na 2 CO 3 solution containing 0.019 g of H 2 PtCl 6 .The above suspension was exposed to the light of a 150 W Hg-lamp, resulting in the photochemical deposition of Pt on K 2 Ti 6 O 13 .Finally, the Pt-loaded K 2 Ti 6 O 13 powders were thoroughly rinsed with distilled water. The prepared Fe-Cu-KDAY and PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 powders were physically mixed by grinding (hereafter, referred to as hybrid catalyst).The prepared catalysts were analyzed using an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, RINT-1400) with Cu K radiation as well as a scanning electron microscope (Hitachi, S-5200).Diffuse reflection spectra were recorded using a UV-Vis spectrometer (Jasco V-550).The BET surface area of the sample powders was measured by the nitrogen adsorption method. The photoreduction experiments of CO 2 with water under Xe-or Hg-lamp irradiation at room temperature were carried out using a photoreactor (173 cm 3 ) equipped with a 63.3 cm 2 flat quartz window.The catalysts were highly dispersed on a filter paper fixed on the bottom of the reactor, into which an appropriate amount of distilled water was introduced.The filter paper acted as a wet bed for the catalyst layer.After degassing using a vacuum system, highly purified CO 2 was introduced into the reactor, which was externally irradiated through the window by a 300 W Xe-lamp and a 150 W Hg-lamp, respectively. Fig. 1 shows a schematic drawing of the experimental system for the photoreduction of CO 2 with water under concentrated sunlight.As illustrated in Fig. 1, the prepared catalysts were placed on wet quartz wool in an optical quartz tube cell (reaction cell), and subsequently an appropriate amount of distilled water was introduced into the cell.In this case, the quartz wool was used as a wet bed for the catalyst layer.After the reaction cell was deaerated using a vacuum system, highly purified CO 2 was introduced into the cell.The cell was tightly closed using a perfluororubber O-ring and no organic contaminants were detected in blank tests performed without the addition of CO 2 .The cell was set on a support bar fixed on an equatorial mount (GPD-type, Vixen, Japan) driven by a sky sensor, which can track the sun automatically.In order to focus the sunlight on the catalyst layer, a concave mirror (diameter 200 mm) was also fixed on the support bar so that the catalyst layer was positioned at the focal point of the mirror.The reaction temperature was monitored with a thermocouple, which was connected to an NR-1000 data acquisition system (Keyence, Japan) and a laptop computer.The experiments were done between 9:30 and 15:30 h on sunny days.Solar insolation upon the reaction cell was measured with a pyronometer (Ishikawa Sangyou Co. Ltd., Japan) H 2 and CH 4 produced in the gas phase were determined using a gas chromatograph (GC) (Shimadzu, GC-14B) equipped with a molecular sieve 5 A column and detected by a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and a flame ionization detector (FID), respectively.Ar was used as the carrier gas.HCOOH produced in the liquid phase was determined using a GC (Shimadzu, GC-6A) equipped with a PEG-6000 column and a TCD, while HCHO was determined using a Porapak T column and a TCD.CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH were determined using a GC (Shimadzu, GC-17A) equipped with a capillary column and an FID.He was used as the carrier gas for the analysis of the products in the liquid phase.The amount of samples taken from the liquid phase for the GC analysis was 10 l.

light source.source: Therefore, it is considered that the prepared hybrid catalyst can produce at least H 2 from water under UV-irradiation, as previously investigated 35.
light source.context: Scanning electron microscope photographs of the prepared catalysts are shown in Fig. 2. The Fe-Cu-K DAY crystals were aggregated into cubic particles of ca.0.5-2 m in size (Fig. 2a).The PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst was composed of rod-like crystallites (Fig. 2b).Fig. 2c shows the morphology of the physical mixture of the two catalysts in a 1:1 weight ratio (hybrid catalyst).As can be seen, the form of the two individual crystals remain unchanged, but the particle size of the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst became smaller due to the grinding, and the surface of the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst was partly covered with the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst. The crystallinity of the prepared samples was examined by XRD analysis.As illustrated in Fig. 3, due to the formation of Fe, Cu, and K components in the cavity as well as on the outer surface of the DAY zeolite, the peak intensity of the DAY zeolite significantly decreased.The BET specific surface areas of the DAY zeolite and Fe-Cu-KDAY were 523.3 and 314.5 m 2 g, respectively, also indicating that the DAY zeolite crystals were covered with the amorphous metal oxide layers.In this case, the DAY zeolite was used as the catalyst support, providing a sufficient surface area for the active metals to disperse and preventing them from sintering.In addition, the OH groups in the DAY zeolite were expected to promote CO 2 uptake on the catalyst.As for the hybrid catalyst, the peak of Fe-Cu-KDAY is hardly seen because the crystallinity of K 2 Ti 6 O 13 is much higher than that of Fe-Cu-KDAY. The light absorption properties of the prepared catalysts were also investigated.Fig. 4 shows the diffuse reflectance spectra of K 2 Ti 6 O 13 (a), the Pt-loaded with Fe-Cu-KDAY has no significant effect on the light absorption behavior of K 2 Ti 6 O 13 , because the absorption edge of the hybrid catalyst is shifted to neither the blue nor the red as compared with that of K 2 Ti 6 O 13 .Therefore, it is considered that the prepared hybrid catalyst can produce at least H 2 from water under UV-irradiation, as previously investigated 35. Table 1 shows the results of the photoreduction of CO 2 with water under Xe-or Hg-lamp irradiation.Each experiment was carried out using 0.3 g of catalyst with 77 kPa of CO 2 and 4.0 ml of distilled water for 6 h in the photoreactor described earlier at room temperature.As shown in Table 1, no reaction products were detected when the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst was exposed to the UV-irradiation from the Hg-lamp.It is suggested that the prepared Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst has no photocatalytic activity for the reduction of CO 2 with water.On the contrary, the evolution of H 2 and CH 4 in the gas phase as well as HCOOH and HCHO in the liquid phase were confirmed for the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst under Hg-lamp irradiation.However, when irradiated with a Xe-lamp, only the evolution of H 2 and CH 4 was observed for PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 .As considered above, this is because the prepared PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst has photocatalytic reactivity in the wavelength regions of 300-420 nm under Xe-lamp irradiation, and wavelengths longer than 420 nm cannot be utilized for the reaction.Note that 33,34.This may account for the reason why no detectable amount of O 2 was formed in this study.On the other hand, the yields of products decreased when the hybrid catalyst was used under both Xe-and Hg-lamp irradiation.The decrease in activity observed here might be due to the light shadowing effect of the Fe-Cu-KDAY powders on the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 powders in the hybrid catalyst, because the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst has no photocatalytic activity and the light incident on Fe-Cu-KDAY is not utilized for the photocatalytic reaction, but lost as heat.It should be also taken into consideration that for Fe-based catalysts, the CO 2 hydrogenation proceeds via the conversion of iron into iron carbide (mainly composed of the Hagg carbide, X-Fe 5 C 2 ) in the presence of CO 2 .However, a relatively high temperature (ca.573 K) is necessary for this conversion 2122232425262728, thus, at room temperature, the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst shows no effects on the reduction of CO 2 .We next performed the reduction of CO 2 with water over the hybrid catalyst using concentrated sunlight, which can supply photons and thermal energy required for the water decomposition and CO 2 hydrogenation over the hybrid catalyst to occur. As mentioned earlier, Fe-based catalysts exhibit catalytic activity for the CO 2 hydrogenation only at relatively high temperatures.We first investigated the effect of temperature on the reduction of CO 2 with water over the hybrid catalyst using Fe-Cu-KDAY and PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 in a 1:1 weight ratio under concentrated sunlight.Table 2 shows the obtained results for various months.In addition, Fig. 5 shows the variations in temperature in the reaction cell under concentrated sunlight with respect to the various experimental days noted in Table 2. Some probabilistic factors, such as wind velocity and humidity, affect the intensity of solar energy received by the cell, resulting in the fluctuation of the temperature in the cell during the experimental periods.It should be noted that the maximum temperature reached ca.600 K in October and 540 K in December.At such relatively high temperatures, the catalytic activity, especially of the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst, would be enhanced to a much greater extent than that at room temperature.Apparently, as shown in Table 2, the species and yields of organic products increased as compared to the results shown in Table 1.When the average reaction temperature was 534 K, HCHO and CH 4 were formed, while traces of HCOOH and CH 3 OH were detected.However, as the average temperature increased to 562 K, the yields of HCOOH, CH 3 OH, and C 2 H 5 OH significantly increased.Moreover, at 590 K, these yields increased two times over those obtained at 562 K. Likewise, the yield of CH 4 also increased with the increasing reaction temperature.Since a concave mirror was used in this study to concentrate the sunlight, both the thermal and photon energy of the sunlight might simultaneously increase, and concentrated sunlight was expected to more efficiently facilitate the reduction of CO 2 into organic compounds than the normal sunlight.The increase in the yields observed here might be due to the heat-enhanced reduction of CO 2 over the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst as well as the photo-enhanced hydrogenation of CO 2 over the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst under concentrated sunlight.In the case of a photocatalyst, in general, light irradiation is the primary source of the energy for the formation of electron-hole pairs at ambient temperature, because the band gap energy is too high to be overcome by thermal excitation.However, at a higher temperature, an increase in the collision frequency and diffusion rate can lead to an increase in the reaction rate.Fujii and Matsuda 36 reported that boiling the reaction solution by heating at 373 K led to a remarkable improvement in the activity of the photocatalytic decomposition of water.These findings indicate that the photocatalytic activity could be enhanced by supplying heat.On the other hand, in the case of the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst, which is not a semiconductor, like other FT iron-based catalysts, its catalytic activity is basically enhanced by heat.However, its reaction activity is considered to be also improved by light irradiation because the ultraviolet photon having relatively high energy, which is included in sunlight, can be absorbed by the valence electrons of any materials, which may assist in generating electronic defect sites for valence level excitation of the adsorbed reactants.Consequently, the yields of organic compounds remarkably increased under concentrated sunlight in the present study. Comparative tests were also conducted on the reduction of CO 2 with water over the hybrid catalyst using Fe-Cu-KDAY and PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 in various weight ratios under concentrated sunlight.As discussed previously, many factors affect the intensity of the solar energy received by the catalysts, depending on experimental days, which in turn result in variations in the average reaction temperature.Hence, we used the experimental results having the average reaction temperature of 579-590 K for comparisons from all the obtained results.The extracted data are shown in Table 3.It is noticeable that the yields of CH 4 and HCOOH and HCHO are higher at the reaction temperature of 583 K for the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst than those at the room temperature shown in Table 1.It also indicates that the photoreduction activity of PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 increased with the increasing reaction temperature.However, no CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH were detected even at a high temperature over PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 . From these results, we can conclude that CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH are catalytically formed over the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst.On the contrary, when only the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst was used, no product was detected even after the prolonged irradiation of concentrated sunlight.It follows that H 2 as the reducing agent is indispensable for the CO 2 reduction over the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst.We also observed that the decrease in CH 4 , HCOOH, and HCHO yields for the hybrid catalysts occurred with the increase in the weight ratio of Fe-Cu-KDAY to PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 , as shown in Table 3.As stated in the previous section, the shadowing effect of the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst on the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst would prevail when increasing the weight ratio of Fe-Cu-KDAY in the hybrid catalyst.This means that the number of incident photons reaching K 2 Ti 6 O 13 becomes less for the hybrid catalyst, thereby reducing the number of electrons and holes available for the reaction.Therefore, the decrease in the yields of the photoreduction products, including H 2 , CH 4 , HCOOH and HCHO, over PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 is responsible for the decrease in the yields for the hybrid catalyst.Inui and coworkers 25,26 have reported that the Fe-based catalysts exhibited high catalytic reactivity for the formation of C C bond on its surface, and that alcohols such as CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH were always easily formed in the CO 2 hydrogenation process.In the present study, DAY was used as a catalyst support instead of Al 2 O 3 .As discussed earlier in terms of the results shown in Tables 123, we found that the combination of Fe-Cu-KDAY with PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 led to the formation of CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH under concentrated sunlight.However, if the weight ratio of Fe-Cu-KDAY in the hybrid catalyst inappropriately increased (e.g.2:1 weight ratio in Table 3), the formation of CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH could be suppressed, because a serious shadowing effect of the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst may result in no H 2 evolution over the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst, or an insufficient amount of H 2 produced for the CO 2 hydrogenation over the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst.Moreover, it is noteworthy that the reaction temperature reached in the present study was relatively low in comparison with that reported by Inui and coworkers 25,26.We rationalize here the enhanced catalytic activity of the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst as follows: (i) DAY promotes CO 2 dissociative chemisorption on the Fe-Cu-KDAY surface because of the OH groups adsorbed in DAY, (ii) iron carbide, presumably reduction sites for CO 2 , is more easily formed and activated under the irradiation of concentrated sunlight than in the dark due to the photo-enhancement effect as mentioned earlier, and (iii) H 2 for the CO 2 hydrogenation is provided by the water decomposition over the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst.It is well known that the H 2 spillover phenomenon forming active atomic H easily occurs on the Pt metals loaded on a catalyst 37.Therefore, it is possible that atomic H formed on Pt, which is loaded on K 2 Ti 6 O 13 , moves to the neighboring surface of Fe-Cu-KDAY with reduced difficulty.This may assist in decreasing thermal energy needed for the CO 2 hydrogenation reaction over the hybrid catalyst; accordingly, CO 2 is reduced to CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH under the rather mild conditions employed in this study. In order to investigate in more detail the effect of the combination of the two catalysts, Fe-Cu-KDAY and PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 , on the reduction of CO 2 with water, the two catalysts were packed in series inside the reactor cell in a 1:1 weight ratio.As a result, the yields of CH 4 , HCOOH, and HCHO increased, but those of CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH decreased compared to that obtained when physically mixed, as shown in Table 3.This indicates that the shadowing effect of Fe-Cu-KDAY on PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 disappears when packed in series, but the transport of the active species, atomic H, resulting from H 2 spillover on PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 to Fe-Cu-KDAY becomes difficult. The obtained results revealed that the combination of the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst and the Fe-Cu-K DAY CO 2 hydrogenation catalyst was effective for synthesizing CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH from CO 2 and water under concentrated sunlight.However, the amount of obtained organic products was low; the apparent turnover number (TON), defined as the ratio of the total amount of CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH produced to that of the active site of Fe in the hybrid catalyst was 0.05 when used at 590 K for 6 h.This low TON value observed in this study must be due to the low activity of the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst for H 2 production, though the TON for H 2 production (the amount of H 2 formed to that of Pt in the hybrid catalyst) exceeded unity.Thus, more active photocatalysts, which is able to supply a sufficient amount of H 2 for the CO 2 hydrogenation over Fe-Cu-KDAY to proceed at high rate, should be developed to improve the CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH yields in this reaction system.

that the decrease in CH 4 , HCOOH, and HCHO yields for the hybrid catalysts occurred with the increase in the weight ratio of Fe-Cu-KDAY to PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 , as shown in Table 3.As stated in the previous section, the shadowing effect of the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst on the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst would prevail when increasing the weight ratio of Fe-Cu-KDAY in the hybrid catalyst.This means that the number of incident photons reaching K 2 Ti 6 O 13 becomes less for the hybrid catalyst, thereby reducing the number of electrons and holes available for the reaction.Therefore, the decrease in the yields of the photoreduction products, including H 2 , CH 4 , HCOOH and HCHO, over PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 is responsible for the decrease in the yields for the hybrid catalyst.Inui and coworkers 25,26 have reported that the Fe-based catalysts exhibited high catalytic reactivity for the formation of C C bond on its surface, and that alcohols such as CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH were always easily formed in the CO 2 hydrogenation process.In the present study, DAY was used as a catalyst support instead of Al 2 O 3 .As discussed earlier in terms of the results shown in Tables 123, we found that the combination of Fe-Cu-KDAY with PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 led to the formation of CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH under concentrated sunlight.However, if the weight ratio of Fe-Cu-KDAY in the hybrid catalyst inappropriately increased (e.g.2:1 weight ratio in Table 3), the formation of CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH could be suppressed, because a serious shadowing effect of the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst may result in no H 2 evolution over the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst, or an insufficient amount of H 2 produced for the CO 2 hydrogenation over the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst.Moreover, it is noteworthy that the reaction temperature reached in the present study was relatively low in comparison with that reported by Inui and coworkers 25,26.We rationalize here the enhanced catalytic activity of the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst as follows: (i) DAY promotes CO 2 dissociative chemisorption on the Fe-Cu-KDAY surface because of the OH groups adsorbed in DAY, (ii) iron carbide, presumably reduction sites for CO 2 , is more easily formed and activated under the irradiation of concentrated sunlight than in the dark due to the photo-enhancement effect as mentioned earlier, and (iii) H 2 for the CO 2 hydrogenation is provided by the water decomposition over the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst.It is well known that the H 2 spillover phenomenon forming active atomic H easily occurs on the Pt metals loaded on a catalyst 37.Therefore, it is possible that atomic H formed on Pt, which is loaded on K 2 Ti 6 O 13 , moves to the neighboring surface of Fe-Cu-KDAY with reduced difficulty.This may assist in decreasing thermal energy needed for the CO 2 hydrogenation reaction over the hybrid catalyst; accordingly, CO 2 is reduced to CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH under the rather mild conditions employed in this study. In order to investigate in more detail the effect of the combination of the two catalysts, Fe-Cu-KDAY and PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 , on the reduction of CO 2 with water, the two catalysts were packed in series inside the reactor cell in a 1:1 weight ratio.As a result, the yields of CH 4 , HCOOH, and HCHO increased, but those of CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH decreased compared to that obtained when physically mixed, as shown in Table 3.This indicates that the shadowing effect of Fe-Cu-KDAY on PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 disappears when packed in series, but the transport of the active species, atomic H, resulting from H 2 spillover on PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 to Fe-Cu-KDAY becomes difficult. The obtained results revealed that the combination of the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst and the Fe-Cu-K DAY CO 2 hydrogenation catalyst was effective for synthesizing CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH from CO 2 and water under concentrated sunlight.However, the amount of obtained organic products was low; the apparent turnover number (TON), defined as the ratio of the total amount of CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH produced to that of the active site of Fe in the hybrid catalyst was 0.05 when used at 590 K for 6 h.This low TON value observed in this study must be due to the low activity of the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst for H 2 production, though the TON for H 2 production (the amount of H 2 formed to that of Pt in the hybrid catalyst) exceeded unity.Thus, more active photocatalysts, which is able to supply a sufficient amount of H 2 for the CO 2 hydrogenation over Fe-Cu-KDAY to proceed at high rate, should be developed to improve the CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH yields in this reaction system.

light source.source: The reduction of CO 2 with water under concentrated sunlight over the hybrid catalyst, in which the Pt-loaded K 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst was combined with the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst, resulted in the formation of organic compounds such as CH 4 , HCOOH, HCHO, CH 3 OH, and C 2 H 5 OH.
light source.context: The reduction of CO 2 with water under concentrated sunlight over the hybrid catalyst, in which the Pt-loaded K 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst was combined with the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst, resulted in the formation of organic compounds such as CH 4 , HCOOH, HCHO, CH 3 OH, and C 2 H 5 OH.The formation of C 2 H 5 OH in this reaction system is worth noting, although the yield needs to be significantly improved.On the other hand, when only the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst was used in the absence of the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst, no products were detected.This supports the idea that the H 2 resulting from the water decomposition over the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst serves as the reducing agent for the CO 2 hydrogenation over the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst, indicating the effectiveness of the combination of the above two catalysts for CO 2 reduction.The reaction temperature reached 590 K by concentrating the solar radiation; such a high temperature promotes the CO 2 hydrogenation over the hybrid catalyst.It was also found that the activity of the photocatalyst for CO 2 reduction was improved under concentrated sunlight presumably by the simultaneous supply of photons and thermal energy.This study suggests that photocatalytic processes utilizing concentrated sunlight have the potential for organic compound syntheses from CO 2 and water without excessive energy. lamp.source: The above suspension was exposed to the light of a 150 W Hg-lamp, resulting in the photochemical deposition of Pt on K 2 Ti 6 O 13 .
lamp.context: The chemicals used in the present study were of reagent grade, purchased from Wako Pure Chemical Industry, and used without further purification.The dealuminized Y-type zeolite (DAY zeolite), with a SiAl ratio of 11.4, was prepared by steam dealumination of a commercial Y-type zeolite (Catalysts Chemicals Industry Co. Ltd., Japan), which was ion exchanged with NH 4 prior to use.The Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst with the composition of 1.0Fe:0.03Cu:0.5K:11.0DAY (weight ratio) was prepared by impregnation.DAY zeolite powders were impregnated in a mixed solution of metal nitrites at the designated ratio while stirring.The resulting powder samples were then dried overnight at 373 K, followed by calcination at 673 K for 2 h in a stream of O 2 at a flow rate of 30 mlmin, and subsequently reduced at 673 K for 2 h in H 2 (20 mlmin). The Pt-loaded K 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst was synthesized as follows: 5.0 g of TiO 2 (P-25) powder was dispersed in distilled water containing 1.4 g of K 2 CO 3 at room temperature.The resulting samples were dried and then calcined at 1213 K for 20 h in air.Pt was loaded on the K 2 Ti 6 O 13 powder by photochemical deposition.3.0 g of K 2 Ti 6 O 13 powder was dispersed in 60 ml of 2 M Na 2 CO 3 solution containing 0.019 g of H 2 PtCl 6 .The above suspension was exposed to the light of a 150 W Hg-lamp, resulting in the photochemical deposition of Pt on K 2 Ti 6 O 13 .Finally, the Pt-loaded K 2 Ti 6 O 13 powders were thoroughly rinsed with distilled water. The prepared Fe-Cu-KDAY and PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 powders were physically mixed by grinding (hereafter, referred to as hybrid catalyst).The prepared catalysts were analyzed using an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, RINT-1400) with Cu K radiation as well as a scanning electron microscope (Hitachi, S-5200).Diffuse reflection spectra were recorded using a UV-Vis spectrometer (Jasco V-550).The BET surface area of the sample powders was measured by the nitrogen adsorption method. The photoreduction experiments of CO 2 with water under Xe-or Hg-lamp irradiation at room temperature were carried out using a photoreactor (173 cm 3 ) equipped with a 63.3 cm 2 flat quartz window.The catalysts were highly dispersed on a filter paper fixed on the bottom of the reactor, into which an appropriate amount of distilled water was introduced.The filter paper acted as a wet bed for the catalyst layer.After degassing using a vacuum system, highly purified CO 2 was introduced into the reactor, which was externally irradiated through the window by a 300 W Xe-lamp and a 150 W Hg-lamp, respectively. Fig. 1 shows a schematic drawing of the experimental system for the photoreduction of CO 2 with water under concentrated sunlight.As illustrated in Fig. 1, the prepared catalysts were placed on wet quartz wool in an optical quartz tube cell (reaction cell), and subsequently an appropriate amount of distilled water was introduced into the cell.In this case, the quartz wool was used as a wet bed for the catalyst layer.After the reaction cell was deaerated using a vacuum system, highly purified CO 2 was introduced into the cell.The cell was tightly closed using a perfluororubber O-ring and no organic contaminants were detected in blank tests performed without the addition of CO 2 .The cell was set on a support bar fixed on an equatorial mount (GPD-type, Vixen, Japan) driven by a sky sensor, which can track the sun automatically.In order to focus the sunlight on the catalyst layer, a concave mirror (diameter 200 mm) was also fixed on the support bar so that the catalyst layer was positioned at the focal point of the mirror.The reaction temperature was monitored with a thermocouple, which was connected to an NR-1000 data acquisition system (Keyence, Japan) and a laptop computer.The experiments were done between 9:30 and 15:30 h on sunny days.Solar insolation upon the reaction cell was measured with a pyronometer (Ishikawa Sangyou Co. Ltd., Japan) H 2 and CH 4 produced in the gas phase were determined using a gas chromatograph (GC) (Shimadzu, GC-14B) equipped with a molecular sieve 5 A column and detected by a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and a flame ionization detector (FID), respectively.Ar was used as the carrier gas.HCOOH produced in the liquid phase was determined using a GC (Shimadzu, GC-6A) equipped with a PEG-6000 column and a TCD, while HCHO was determined using a Porapak T column and a TCD.CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH were determined using a GC (Shimadzu, GC-17A) equipped with a capillary column and an FID.He was used as the carrier gas for the analysis of the products in the liquid phase.The amount of samples taken from the liquid phase for the GC analysis was 10 l.

lamp.source: Table 1 shows the results of the photoreduction of CO 2 with water under Xe-or Hg-lamp irradiation.
lamp.context: Scanning electron microscope photographs of the prepared catalysts are shown in Fig. 2. The Fe-Cu-K DAY crystals were aggregated into cubic particles of ca.0.5-2 m in size (Fig. 2a).The PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst was composed of rod-like crystallites (Fig. 2b).Fig. 2c shows the morphology of the physical mixture of the two catalysts in a 1:1 weight ratio (hybrid catalyst).As can be seen, the form of the two individual crystals remain unchanged, but the particle size of the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst became smaller due to the grinding, and the surface of the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst was partly covered with the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst. The crystallinity of the prepared samples was examined by XRD analysis.As illustrated in Fig. 3, due to the formation of Fe, Cu, and K components in the cavity as well as on the outer surface of the DAY zeolite, the peak intensity of the DAY zeolite significantly decreased.The BET specific surface areas of the DAY zeolite and Fe-Cu-KDAY were 523.3 and 314.5 m 2 g, respectively, also indicating that the DAY zeolite crystals were covered with the amorphous metal oxide layers.In this case, the DAY zeolite was used as the catalyst support, providing a sufficient surface area for the active metals to disperse and preventing them from sintering.In addition, the OH groups in the DAY zeolite were expected to promote CO 2 uptake on the catalyst.As for the hybrid catalyst, the peak of Fe-Cu-KDAY is hardly seen because the crystallinity of K 2 Ti 6 O 13 is much higher than that of Fe-Cu-KDAY. The light absorption properties of the prepared catalysts were also investigated.Fig. 4 shows the diffuse reflectance spectra of K 2 Ti 6 O 13 (a), the Pt-loaded with Fe-Cu-KDAY has no significant effect on the light absorption behavior of K 2 Ti 6 O 13 , because the absorption edge of the hybrid catalyst is shifted to neither the blue nor the red as compared with that of K 2 Ti 6 O 13 .Therefore, it is considered that the prepared hybrid catalyst can produce at least H 2 from water under UV-irradiation, as previously investigated 35. Table 1 shows the results of the photoreduction of CO 2 with water under Xe-or Hg-lamp irradiation.Each experiment was carried out using 0.3 g of catalyst with 77 kPa of CO 2 and 4.0 ml of distilled water for 6 h in the photoreactor described earlier at room temperature.As shown in Table 1, no reaction products were detected when the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst was exposed to the UV-irradiation from the Hg-lamp.It is suggested that the prepared Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst has no photocatalytic activity for the reduction of CO 2 with water.On the contrary, the evolution of H 2 and CH 4 in the gas phase as well as HCOOH and HCHO in the liquid phase were confirmed for the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst under Hg-lamp irradiation.However, when irradiated with a Xe-lamp, only the evolution of H 2 and CH 4 was observed for PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 .As considered above, this is because the prepared PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst has photocatalytic reactivity in the wavelength regions of 300-420 nm under Xe-lamp irradiation, and wavelengths longer than 420 nm cannot be utilized for the reaction.Note that 33,34.This may account for the reason why no detectable amount of O 2 was formed in this study.On the other hand, the yields of products decreased when the hybrid catalyst was used under both Xe-and Hg-lamp irradiation.The decrease in activity observed here might be due to the light shadowing effect of the Fe-Cu-KDAY powders on the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 powders in the hybrid catalyst, because the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst has no photocatalytic activity and the light incident on Fe-Cu-KDAY is not utilized for the photocatalytic reaction, but lost as heat.It should be also taken into consideration that for Fe-based catalysts, the CO 2 hydrogenation proceeds via the conversion of iron into iron carbide (mainly composed of the Hagg carbide, X-Fe 5 C 2 ) in the presence of CO 2 .However, a relatively high temperature (ca.573 K) is necessary for this conversion 2122232425262728, thus, at room temperature, the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst shows no effects on the reduction of CO 2 .We next performed the reduction of CO 2 with water over the hybrid catalyst using concentrated sunlight, which can supply photons and thermal energy required for the water decomposition and CO 2 hydrogenation over the hybrid catalyst to occur. As mentioned earlier, Fe-based catalysts exhibit catalytic activity for the CO 2 hydrogenation only at relatively high temperatures.We first investigated the effect of temperature on the reduction of CO 2 with water over the hybrid catalyst using Fe-Cu-KDAY and PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 in a 1:1 weight ratio under concentrated sunlight.Table 2 shows the obtained results for various months.In addition, Fig. 5 shows the variations in temperature in the reaction cell under concentrated sunlight with respect to the various experimental days noted in Table 2. Some probabilistic factors, such as wind velocity and humidity, affect the intensity of solar energy received by the cell, resulting in the fluctuation of the temperature in the cell during the experimental periods.It should be noted that the maximum temperature reached ca.600 K in October and 540 K in December.At such relatively high temperatures, the catalytic activity, especially of the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst, would be enhanced to a much greater extent than that at room temperature.Apparently, as shown in Table 2, the species and yields of organic products increased as compared to the results shown in Table 1.When the average reaction temperature was 534 K, HCHO and CH 4 were formed, while traces of HCOOH and CH 3 OH were detected.However, as the average temperature increased to 562 K, the yields of HCOOH, CH 3 OH, and C 2 H 5 OH significantly increased.Moreover, at 590 K, these yields increased two times over those obtained at 562 K. Likewise, the yield of CH 4 also increased with the increasing reaction temperature.Since a concave mirror was used in this study to concentrate the sunlight, both the thermal and photon energy of the sunlight might simultaneously increase, and concentrated sunlight was expected to more efficiently facilitate the reduction of CO 2 into organic compounds than the normal sunlight.The increase in the yields observed here might be due to the heat-enhanced reduction of CO 2 over the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst as well as the photo-enhanced hydrogenation of CO 2 over the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst under concentrated sunlight.In the case of a photocatalyst, in general, light irradiation is the primary source of the energy for the formation of electron-hole pairs at ambient temperature, because the band gap energy is too high to be overcome by thermal excitation.However, at a higher temperature, an increase in the collision frequency and diffusion rate can lead to an increase in the reaction rate.Fujii and Matsuda 36 reported that boiling the reaction solution by heating at 373 K led to a remarkable improvement in the activity of the photocatalytic decomposition of water.These findings indicate that the photocatalytic activity could be enhanced by supplying heat.On the other hand, in the case of the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst, which is not a semiconductor, like other FT iron-based catalysts, its catalytic activity is basically enhanced by heat.However, its reaction activity is considered to be also improved by light irradiation because the ultraviolet photon having relatively high energy, which is included in sunlight, can be absorbed by the valence electrons of any materials, which may assist in generating electronic defect sites for valence level excitation of the adsorbed reactants.Consequently, the yields of organic compounds remarkably increased under concentrated sunlight in the present study. Comparative tests were also conducted on the reduction of CO 2 with water over the hybrid catalyst using Fe-Cu-KDAY and PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 in various weight ratios under concentrated sunlight.As discussed previously, many factors affect the intensity of the solar energy received by the catalysts, depending on experimental days, which in turn result in variations in the average reaction temperature.Hence, we used the experimental results having the average reaction temperature of 579-590 K for comparisons from all the obtained results.The extracted data are shown in Table 3.It is noticeable that the yields of CH 4 and HCOOH and HCHO are higher at the reaction temperature of 583 K for the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst than those at the room temperature shown in Table 1.It also indicates that the photoreduction activity of PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 increased with the increasing reaction temperature.However, no CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH were detected even at a high temperature over PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 . From these results, we can conclude that CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH are catalytically formed over the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst.On the contrary, when only the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst was used, no product was detected even after the prolonged irradiation of concentrated sunlight.It follows that H 2 as the reducing agent is indispensable for the CO 2 reduction over the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst.We also observed that the decrease in CH 4 , HCOOH, and HCHO yields for the hybrid catalysts occurred with the increase in the weight ratio of Fe-Cu-KDAY to PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 , as shown in Table 3.As stated in the previous section, the shadowing effect of the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst on the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst would prevail when increasing the weight ratio of Fe-Cu-KDAY in the hybrid catalyst.This means that the number of incident photons reaching K 2 Ti 6 O 13 becomes less for the hybrid catalyst, thereby reducing the number of electrons and holes available for the reaction.Therefore, the decrease in the yields of the photoreduction products, including H 2 , CH 4 , HCOOH and HCHO, over PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 is responsible for the decrease in the yields for the hybrid catalyst.Inui and coworkers 25,26 have reported that the Fe-based catalysts exhibited high catalytic reactivity for the formation of C C bond on its surface, and that alcohols such as CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH were always easily formed in the CO 2 hydrogenation process.In the present study, DAY was used as a catalyst support instead of Al 2 O 3 .As discussed earlier in terms of the results shown in Tables 123, we found that the combination of Fe-Cu-KDAY with PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 led to the formation of CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH under concentrated sunlight.However, if the weight ratio of Fe-Cu-KDAY in the hybrid catalyst inappropriately increased (e.g.2:1 weight ratio in Table 3), the formation of CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH could be suppressed, because a serious shadowing effect of the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst may result in no H 2 evolution over the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst, or an insufficient amount of H 2 produced for the CO 2 hydrogenation over the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst.Moreover, it is noteworthy that the reaction temperature reached in the present study was relatively low in comparison with that reported by Inui and coworkers 25,26.We rationalize here the enhanced catalytic activity of the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst as follows: (i) DAY promotes CO 2 dissociative chemisorption on the Fe-Cu-KDAY surface because of the OH groups adsorbed in DAY, (ii) iron carbide, presumably reduction sites for CO 2 , is more easily formed and activated under the irradiation of concentrated sunlight than in the dark due to the photo-enhancement effect as mentioned earlier, and (iii) H 2 for the CO 2 hydrogenation is provided by the water decomposition over the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst.It is well known that the H 2 spillover phenomenon forming active atomic H easily occurs on the Pt metals loaded on a catalyst 37.Therefore, it is possible that atomic H formed on Pt, which is loaded on K 2 Ti 6 O 13 , moves to the neighboring surface of Fe-Cu-KDAY with reduced difficulty.This may assist in decreasing thermal energy needed for the CO 2 hydrogenation reaction over the hybrid catalyst; accordingly, CO 2 is reduced to CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH under the rather mild conditions employed in this study. In order to investigate in more detail the effect of the combination of the two catalysts, Fe-Cu-KDAY and PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 , on the reduction of CO 2 with water, the two catalysts were packed in series inside the reactor cell in a 1:1 weight ratio.As a result, the yields of CH 4 , HCOOH, and HCHO increased, but those of CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH decreased compared to that obtained when physically mixed, as shown in Table 3.This indicates that the shadowing effect of Fe-Cu-KDAY on PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 disappears when packed in series, but the transport of the active species, atomic H, resulting from H 2 spillover on PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 to Fe-Cu-KDAY becomes difficult. The obtained results revealed that the combination of the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst and the Fe-Cu-K DAY CO 2 hydrogenation catalyst was effective for synthesizing CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH from CO 2 and water under concentrated sunlight.However, the amount of obtained organic products was low; the apparent turnover number (TON), defined as the ratio of the total amount of CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH produced to that of the active site of Fe in the hybrid catalyst was 0.05 when used at 590 K for 6 h.This low TON value observed in this study must be due to the low activity of the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst for H 2 production, though the TON for H 2 production (the amount of H 2 formed to that of Pt in the hybrid catalyst) exceeded unity.Thus, more active photocatalysts, which is able to supply a sufficient amount of H 2 for the CO 2 hydrogenation over Fe-Cu-KDAY to proceed at high rate, should be developed to improve the CH 3 OH and C 2 H 5 OH yields in this reaction system.

lamp.context: The reduction of CO 2 with water under concentrated sunlight over the hybrid catalyst, in which the Pt-loaded K 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst was combined with the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst, resulted in the formation of organic compounds such as CH 4 , HCOOH, HCHO, CH 3 OH, and C 2 H 5 OH.The formation of C 2 H 5 OH in this reaction system is worth noting, although the yield needs to be significantly improved.On the other hand, when only the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst was used in the absence of the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst, no products were detected.This supports the idea that the H 2 resulting from the water decomposition over the PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst serves as the reducing agent for the CO 2 hydrogenation over the Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst, indicating the effectiveness of the combination of the above two catalysts for CO 2 reduction.The reaction temperature reached 590 K by concentrating the solar radiation; such a high temperature promotes the CO 2 hydrogenation over the hybrid catalyst.It was also found that the activity of the photocatalyst for CO 2 reduction was improved under concentrated sunlight presumably by the simultaneous supply of photons and thermal energy.This study suggests that photocatalytic processes utilizing concentrated sunlight have the potential for organic compound syntheses from CO 2 and water without excessive energy. reaction medium.source: H 2 and CH 4 produced in the gas phase were determined using a gas chromatograph (GC) (Shimadzu, GC-14B) equipped with a molecular sieve 5 A column and detected by a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and a flame ionization detector (FID), respectively.
reaction medium.context: The chemicals used in the present study were of reagent grade, purchased from Wako Pure Chemical Industry, and used without further purification.The dealuminized Y-type zeolite (DAY zeolite), with a SiAl ratio of 11.4, was prepared by steam dealumination of a commercial Y-type zeolite (Catalysts Chemicals Industry Co. Ltd., Japan), which was ion exchanged with NH 4 prior to use.The Fe-Cu-KDAY catalyst with the composition of 1.0Fe:0.03Cu:0.5K:11.0DAY (weight ratio) was prepared by impregnation.DAY zeolite powders were impregnated in a mixed solution of metal nitrites at the designated ratio while stirring.The resulting powder samples were then dried overnight at 373 K, followed by calcination at 673 K for 2 h in a stream of O 2 at a flow rate of 30 mlmin, and subsequently reduced at 673 K for 2 h in H 2 (20 mlmin). The Pt-loaded K 2 Ti 6 O 13 photocatalyst was synthesized as follows: 5.0 g of TiO 2 (P-25) powder was dispersed in distilled water containing 1.4 g of K 2 CO 3 at room temperature.The resulting samples were dried and then calcined at 1213 K for 20 h in air.Pt was loaded on the K 2 Ti 6 O 13 powder by photochemical deposition.3.0 g of K 2 Ti 6 O 13 powder was dispersed in 60 ml of 2 M Na 2 CO 3 solution containing 0.019 g of H 2 PtCl 6 .The above suspension was exposed to the light of a 150 W Hg-lamp, resulting in the photochemical deposition of Pt on K 2 Ti 6 O 13 .Finally, the Pt-loaded K 2 Ti 6 O 13 powders were thoroughly rinsed with distilled water. The prepared Fe-Cu-KDAY and PtK 2 Ti 6 O 13 powders were physically mixed by grinding (hereafter, referred to as hybrid catalyst).The prepared catalysts were analyzed using an X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, RINT-1400) with Cu K radiation as well as a scanning electron microscope (Hitachi, S-5200).Diffuse reflection spectra were recorded using a UV-Vis spectrometer (Jasco V-550).The BET surface area of the sample powders was measured by the nitrogen adsorption method. The photoreduction experiments of CO 2 with water under Xe-or Hg-lamp irradiation at room temperature were carried out using a photoreactor (173 cm 3 ) equipped with a 63.3 cm 2 flat quartz window.The catalysts were highly dispersed on a filter paper fixed on the bottom of the reactor, into which an appropriate amount of distilled water was introduced.The filter paper acted as a wet bed for the catalyst layer.After degassing using a vacuum system, highly purified CO 2 was introduced into the reactor, which was externally irradiated through the window by a 300 W Xe-lamp and a 150 W Hg-lamp, respectively.
